title: Composition-Weighted Symbolic Regression for General-Purpose Property Prediction
abstract: We introduce a composition-weighted symbolic regression framework for interpretable prediction of materials properties directly from chemical composition. The method jointly learns analytical functional forms and task-dependent elemental weightings without predefined descriptors. By incorporating max/min operators, it naturally enforces constraints such as non-negative band gaps and bounded classification probabilities, unifying regression and classification tasks. Efficient search is achieved through a hybrid Monte Carlo tree search--genetic programming algorithm with gradient-based refinement and parallel computation. Benchmarks on MatBench tasks show competitive accuracy relative to state-of-the-art black-box models while yielding explicit analytical expressions. Applied to III--V semiconductor alloys, the model produces smooth composition-dependent trends and learned elemental weights with chemically meaningful periodic behavior. This framework provides a scalable and interpretable route for materials discovery and property screening.
summary: This paper presents a new symbolic regression method that discovers simple mathematical formulas to predict material properties from chemical composition. Unlike deep learning, it provides interpretable equations that capture periodic trends in the periodic table. The model performs as well as complex black-box models while using much less computational memory and parameters.
main_problem: The challenge of predicting materials properties from chemical composition with high accuracy and physical interpretability, avoiding the 'black-box' nature of traditional neural networks.
main_result: The framework achieves competitive accuracy with state-of-the-art models using significantly fewer parameters, providing explicit analytical expressions and recovering periodic chemical trends.
method: A hybrid search algorithm combining Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with Genetic Programming (GP), supplemented by gradient-based refinement using L-BFGS.
model_or_system: A composition-weighted symbolic regression framework that learns analytical functional forms and elemental weights directly from chemical composition without predefined descriptors.
key_observables: Band gap (MAE), metallicity (ROC-AUC), and glass formation (ROC-AUC).
important_parameters: Learnable elemental weights (w_i) for each element in the periodic table and composition-weighted latent descriptors.
main_assumptions: Assumes that physically meaningful governing relations are typically compact and structured, and that the optimization landscape is piecewise differentiable.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the hybrid MCTS-GP framework; Figure 2 visualizes periodic trends in learned elemental weights; Figure 3 shows the relationship between a gating factor and band gap; Figure 4 compares predicted vs. experimental band gaps for III-V alloys.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of interpretable property prediction, details the hybrid MCTS-GP architecture and optimization strategy, presents benchmarks on MatBench tasks, validates the model on III-V semiconductor alloys, and discusses limitations regarding complexity and data dependency.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Hybrid Monte Carlo tree search and genetic programming.

FIG. 3: Scatter plot of x1 versus Fgap/x1 with aligned marginal histograms. Gray points denote zero-gap systems, while colored points represent finite-gap compounds (log scale). The distributions indicate separation between metallic and insulating materials.
FIG. 2: Learned elemental weights visualized on the periodic table for (top) matbench expt gap, (middle) matbench expt is metal, and (bottom) matbench glass.

FIG. 4: Band gap comparison for selected III–V semiconductor alloys (adapted from Ref. [33]). Experimental data (open black circles) and interpolation fits (black solid line) [34–57]; MatBench training data [5] (open orange circles). Predictions: DARWIN [30, 31] (cyan dashed), MODNet [11, 12] (purple dashed), CrabNet [15, 16] (green dashed), the ReLU model [21] (brown dashed) and this work (red solid). Data sources are detailed in the Supporting Materials.
